episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Verge] A $2,000 AI-generated film will make its debut at Tribeca and [Nautilus] This Pope Weighed in on Modern Tech as Media Forever Changed, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural Indigenous communities maintain editorial control and long-term resilience of cultural archives without depending on commercial platforms whose algorithms and policies they cannot control? Topics include: Platform dependency and algorithmic control in content discovery, Open infrastructure alternatives (RSS, local hosting, mesh networks) versus commercial platforms, AI tools in cultural production and the ethics of training data, Indigenous data sovereignty and tiered access protocols, Maintenance capacity as the critical barrier to sustainable community archives.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Station House Gallery

discussion.central_question: How can rural Indigenous communities maintain editorial control and long-term resilience of cultural archives without depending on commercial platforms whose algorithms and policies they cannot control?
discussion.riley_position: Commercial platforms are necessary distribution infrastructure that can coexist with owned distribution channels; communities should use platforms for discovery while maintaining RSS-based ownership, and declining production costs create new opportunities for storytelling.
riley_key_evidence: Canadaland built a multi-show network through direct listener relationships while still distributing on Spotify
The $2,000 AI film at Tribeca demonstrates dramatically lowered production cost floors
Māori-owned data storage network demonstrates Indigenous-controlled infrastructure is achievable
discussion.casey_position: Platform dependency is structurally unequal for under-resourced communities; small producers lack the capacity to adapt to algorithmic changes, and open infrastructure alternatives require maintenance capacity that must be deliberately built rather than assumed.
casey_key_evidence: When Spotify changes its discovery algorithm without notice, small shows disappear from recommendations without adaptation capacity
YouTube has flagged Indigenous language content as non-language through automated systems
The $2,000 AI film cost ignores filmmaker time, archival research, and uncompensated training data sourced from human creative work
discussion.resolution: They converged on a concrete proposal: embed digital archival and maintenance functions into existing regional institutions (arts galleries, libraries, band councils) as operating budgets rather than grant-dependent pilots, using open-source tools like Nextcloud while building collaborative governance with local First Nations through models like Mukurtu.
topics_covered: Platform dependency and algorithmic control in content discovery
Open infrastructure alternatives (RSS, local hosting, mesh networks) versus commercial platforms
AI tools in cultural production and the ethics of training data
Indigenous data sovereignty and tiered access protocols
Maintenance capacity as the critical barrier to sustainable community archives
